source: Kenya RDM 6.4 (2025) -- Other Traffic Control Devices, Tables 2.1, 2.2, 2.3
note: Speed hump geometry extracted from Kenya RDM 6.4 Chapter 2. Source for circular humps is Indian Roads Congress IRC 35:2015 as referenced by RDM 6.4.

circular_humps.notes: Rise less than 100 mm is not effective. Rise greater than 100 mm may cause vehicle damage. For roads with bus traffic, select chord length to achieve acceptable bus passage speed.

trapezoidal_humps.notes: Flat-top length minimum 2.5 m. Extend for bus routes to cover full bus wheelbase. Lorries and buses must drive very slowly over trapezoidal humps.

speed_table.notes: Raised pedestrian crossing. Flat-top length increased to bus wheelbase where bus traffic present.

sinusoidal_hump.notes: S-curve profile. More comfortable for cyclists. Source: TRL Report 377.

transverse_bar_markings.colour: yellow on Class A and B roads, white on other classes

speed_bumps.note: RDM 6.4 Section 2.1 explicitly states that speed breakers (abrupt bumps) are implemented as speed humps or speed bumps. Speed bumps have height 76-152 mm and length 0.3-1.0 m. They reduce speed to 5-10 km/h. RDM 6.4 Section 2.4.2 recommends permanent speed bumps only for local and collector streets with approach speed < 50 km/h. Bumps should not be installed on high-speed roads.
speed_bumps.not_recommended_for: Public roads with bus traffic, emergency routes, or approach speed > 50 km/h
